active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi for exact radius computation from positions
active_paradigms.description: Replace the iterative greedy radius adjustment with direct geometric computation using Voronoi diagrams. For given circle positions, compute scipy.spatial.Voronoi to partition the square. Each circle's maximum radius equals the minimum of: (1) distance to square boundary, and (2) half the distance to its Voronoi cell edges. This gives the exact optimal radii for any position set without iteration. Then search over position parameters to maximize sum. This is fundamentally different from the current iterative approach and guarantees optimal radii for given positions.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through position parameter optimization with exact radius computation
active_paradigms.cautions: Handle Voronoi vertices outside the unit square by clipping to boundaries. Use bounded Voronoi or manually clip cell edges. Ensure positions are not too close together or Voronoi computation may fail.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Delaunay for efficient neighbor-constrained radius computation
active_paradigms.description: Instead of computing distances to all n-1 other circles (O(n²)), use scipy.spatial.Delaunay to identify only the relevant neighbors for each circle. The Delaunay triangulation naturally identifies which circles are close enough to constrain each other's radii. For each circle, compute radius as min(boundary distance, distances to Delaunay neighbors minus their radii). This reduces the radius computation from O(n²) to O(n) per iteration since each circle only has ~6 Delaunay neighbors on average. Iterate until convergence, which will be much faster.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: computational efficiency enabling more pattern exploration and finer convergence
active_paradigms.cautions: Delaunay neighbors may not include all constraining circles near boundaries. Add boundary points to triangulation or explicitly check boundary distances. Handle degenerate cases where points are collinear.

active_paradigms.idea: Corner-first asymmetric construction with explicit radius tiers
active_paradigms.description: Abandon symmetric hexagonal patterns. Instead: (1) Place 4 circles in corners with radius r_corner ~0.15-0.20 (corners geometrically allow larger circles), (2) Place 4-8 circles along edges with radius r_edge ~0.10-0.12, (3) Fill remaining ~14-18 interior positions with smaller circles in hexagonal-like pattern with radius r_interior ~0.08-0.10. Use numpy broadcasting for vectorized distance computation. The key insight is that corners can fit circles ~2x larger than interior due to boundary constraints being less restrictive. Search over the three radius parameters and relative positioning to maximize sum.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by exploiting geometric advantages of corners and edges with variable radii
active_paradigms.cautions: Corner circles must not overlap each other across diagonals. Edge circles must account for both corner neighbors and opposite edge. Use numpy.minimum for element-wise radius constraints from boundaries.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use nonlinear equation solving for mutually tangent circle packing
tried_paradigms.description: Formulate the problem as finding positions where circles are mutually tangent (touching) to maximize radii. Set up a system where each circle touches its neighbors and/or boundaries. Use scipy.optimize.root or scipy.optimize.fsolve to find positions satisfying tangency constraints. The key insight: optimal packings often have circles touching multiple others. Define the system of equations: for each circle pair that should touch, ||c_i - c_j|| = r_i + r_j. Solve this geometric constraint system directly rather than iteratively shrinking.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Find exact tangency relationships that maximize total radii
tried_paradigms.cautions: Need good initial guess for the solver, may need to try multiple tangency graph configurations, ensure solution respects boundary constraints

tried_paradigms.idea: Hexagonal lattice template with single-parameter optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of greedy placement, construct circles using explicit hexagonal lattice positions. For 26 circles, use row pattern [5,4,5,4,5,3] or [4,5,4,5,4,4]. Place circles at lattice positions determined by spacing parameter s. Calculate each circle's max radius based on distance to walls and neighbors. Optimize only the spacing parameter via grid search (not scipy.optimize). This uses global geometric structure rather than sequential greedy decisions.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary metric: sum of radii. Focus on finding optimal lattice spacing that maximizes total radius while maintaining valid packing.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Must calculate max radius correctly for each position - constrained by walls and all previously calculated circles. Use multiple row patterns and pick best result. Handle boundary circles specially - they can touch walls.

tried_paradigms.idea: Corners-last placement with iterative radius expansion
tried_paradigms.description: Reverse the greedy order: place interior circles first (most constrained), then edge circles, finally corner circles. This allows corner circles to expand into available space rather than being fixed small. After initial placement, perform iterative expansion: for each circle, recalculate max possible radius given all others. Repeat until convergence. Corner circles should naturally become larger this way.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary metric: sum of radii. Focus on allowing natural radius growth especially for corner circles.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Interior placement order matters - use hexagonal-inspired positions for interior circles. The iterative expansion must converge - limit iterations. Must handle edge case where circles overlap after repositioning.

tried_paradigms.idea: Equal-circle hexagonal packing with boundary adjustment
tried_paradigms.description: Start with assumption that optimal packing has nearly equal circles. Calculate the maximum equal radius r such that 26 circles fit in hexagonal pattern within unit square. For hexagonal packing: 5 rows with alternating counts. Solve for r analytically: horizontal fit gives r ≈ 1/(2*max_count), vertical fit gives r ≈ 1/(sqrt(3)*(rows-1)+2). Use minimum. Then adjust boundary circles to touch walls, growing their radii slightly. This exploits the mathematical insight that equal circles maximize total radius.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary metric: sum of radii. Focus on maximizing the base radius while fitting all circles, then growing boundary circles.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Equal-circle packing may not exactly fit 26 circles - need to handle the pattern carefully. Boundary adjustment must not cause overlaps. Use multiple row patterns [5,6,5,6,4], [4,5,4,5,4,4] etc. and select best.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.root with joint position-radius tangency equations
tried_paradigms.description: Formulate circle packing as a system of nonlinear equations where each circle must be tangent to at least 3 neighbors or boundaries. Variables are all 78 values (x, y, r for each of 26 circles). For each circle pair, add equation dist(c_i, c_j) = r_i + r_j if they should touch. For boundary circles, add equations like x_i - r_i = 0 or 1 - x_i - r_i = 0. Use the current best hexagonal pattern as initial guess. scipy.optimize.root will solve for exact tangent positions simultaneously, allowing positions to shift from the rigid pattern while maintaining tangency. This extends the successful root approach to optimize positions, not just radii.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary sum_radii by allowing positions to shift from fixed patterns while maintaining optimal tangency
tried_paradigms.cautions: System may be over/under-determined; use least-squares mode 'lm' or add slack variables. Need to identify which circles should be tangent (use Delaunay neighbors from initial pattern). Handle numerical precision carefully near boundaries.

tried_paradigms.idea: Implement greedy maximal circle placement with Apollonian gap filling
tried_paradigms.description: Start by placing 4 large circles in corners (each tangent to two walls). Then iteratively: (1) Identify all gaps formed by triples of circles or circle-wall pairs, (2) For each gap, compute the largest inscribed circle tangent to all three elements using Descartes' circle theorem or geometric intersection, (3) Place the largest gap-filling circle, (4) Repeat until 26 circles placed. This is fundamentally different from pattern-based approaches - it constructs the packing greedily based on actual geometry rather than forcing a predefined pattern. The Apollonian gasket principle ensures dense packing in gaps.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary sum_radii through intelligent gap-filling that adapts to actual space available
tried_paradigms.cautions: Must track all gaps efficiently; gap count grows. Use priority queue for largest-gap-first placement. Handle boundary gaps (circle-circle-wall combinations) separately from interior gaps (circle-circle-circle). Ensure circles don't overlap existing ones during placement.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for constrained fine-tuning
tried_paradigms.description: The failed SLSQP approach used a different algorithm. trust-constr uses trust-region methods that handle nonlinear constraints more robustly. Define objective as sum of radii (78 variables: x, y, r for each circle). Constraints: (1) Non-overlap: ||c_i - c_j||^2 >= (r_i + r_j)^2 for all pairs, (2) Boundary: 0 <= x_i - r_i, x_i + r_i <= 1, same for y, (3) Non-negative radii. Use the current best solution as initial guess. trust-constr builds a trust region model of the objective and constraints, which is more suitable for this highly nonlinear constrained problem than SLSQP's sequential quadratic programming.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary sum_radii by jointly optimizing all positions and radii with proper constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires constraints in specific format (LinearConstraint or NonlinearConstraint). Use sparse constraint Jacobian for efficiency (only O(n) constraints involve each variable). Set appropriate trust region radius and tolerance. May need multiple restarts from different initial patterns.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for direct constrained optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Formulate the problem as a continuous optimization with 78 variables (x, y, r for each of 26 circles). The objective is to maximize sum of radii. Use NonlinearConstraint for boundary constraints (x_i - r_i >= 0, x_i + r_i <= 1, y_i - r_i >= 0, y_i + r_i <= 1) and non-overlap constraints (||c_i - c_j||^2 >= (r_i + r_j)^2). Use the current best solution as initial guess. Trust-constr handles many constraints efficiently using barrier methods.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii directly, with all 78 variables optimized simultaneously
tried_paradigms.cautions: Non-overlap constraints are non-convex; use multiple starting points from different patterns. Set appropriate tolerance (1e-8) and max iterations (1000). The constraint Jacobian can be computed analytically for efficiency.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.differential_evolution for global optimization without local minima traps
tried_paradigms.description: Apply differential evolution to search the full 78-dimensional space globally. Define bounds as [0, 1] for positions and [0.01, 0.5] for radii. Implement a penalty function that returns -sum_radii + large_penalty * constraint_violations. Use strategy='best1bin' with population size ~15*78. This global approach can discover non-hexagonal arrangements that pattern-based methods miss.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: global maximum of sum of radii across all feasible configurations
tried_paradigms.cautions: Set maxiter high enough (5000+) but respect timeout. Use workers=-1 for parallelization. Polish=True to refine final solution with L-BFGS-B. The penalty coefficient must be large enough to ensure feasibility.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.integrate.solve_ivp for physics-based expansion simulation
tried_paradigms.description: Model circles as expanding bubbles under pressure. State vector is 78 variables (positions and radii). Define ODEs where each circle expands (dr/dt = 1) until blocked by boundary or neighbor. When circles touch, they push each other apart. Use solve_ivp with events to detect equilibrium (sum of radii stops changing). This naturally finds dense packings through physical equilibrium.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: equilibrium configuration from physics simulation that maximizes space utilization
tried_paradigms.cautions: Use stiff solver (method='Radau' or 'BDF') since collision dynamics can be stiff. Set appropriate event detection tolerances. Initial placement matters - use hexagonal pattern as starting positions. May need multiple runs with different initial conditions.
